text: The surgeon uses robotic assistance to incise the posterior rectus sheath just lateral to the linea alba, entering the preperitoneal space and bringing down the preperitoneal fat. The incision is made carefully to avoid bowel injury, revealing a prior suture near the hernia sac. The surgeon continues to divide the posterior rectus sheath down to the arcuate line, while attempting to stay extraperitoneal.

The surgeon works on the hernia sac, attempting to bring down a thick sac, though it remains thin. The surgeon navigates outside the hernia sac, aiming for a thicker layer, and sometimes operates subcutaneously. The omentum is observed herniated between the anterior rectus sheath and the rectus muscle, incarcerated in the anterior rectus space.

The surgeon places ports on the left side to access the abdominal wall anterior to the rectus muscle. The omentum, previously stuck within the anterior rectus space, is completely taken down. The surgeon proceeds to take down a large hernia sac, removing about half of it to ensure sufficient material for posterior coverage in case of a large defect between the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon carefully separates the available peritoneum from the right rectus muscle, entering the right retrorectus space. Using a grasper, the posterior rectus sheath is opened along the linea alba, starting at the arcuate line. The incision is made approximately one centimeter away from the linea alba.

The surgeon dissects the areolar space within the posterior rectus space, carefully preserving the fibril areolar tissue anteriorly to maintain the integrity of neurovascular bundles. The dissection extends into the pelvis, developing the space of Retzius. The right round ligament is divided away from the internal ring to prevent injury to the genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve.

The surgeon divides the left round ligament, similar to the previous division of the right round ligament. A limited bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) is performed on the right side to ensure adequate mesh coverage beyond the linea semilunaris in the lower abdomen.

The surgeon divides the posterior rectus sheath approximately one centimeter medial to the linea semilunaris, after pushing the peritoneum off the sheath. The entire defect is visible, and the surgeon prepares to use image inversion to close the defect.

The surgeon uses image inversion to facilitate suturing, focusing on the patient's right rectus complex visible on the left side of the screen. The anti-erectus sheath is separated from the rectus muscle, and the surgeon takes bites of both structures to close the anti-erectus space. Multiple bites are taken from the large hernia sac, and sutures are laid out to evenly distribute tension along the incision, aiding in the re-approximation of the linea alba, including a small umbilical hernia.
The surgeon uses an OV lock 180 suture to close the defect, running the suture back slightly after closure. The image is then reverted to its original orientation.

The surgeon focuses on closing the posterior layer using a V-Lock suture technique. The redundant hernia sac is managed, and the posterior gap is closed without difficulty, with an abundance of tissue available for closure. The closure of the posterior layer is nearly complete.
The surgeon measures the floor space to ensure the mesh will adequately cover the entire area, preventing bowel from entering the retroactive space.

The surgeon measures the defect, which is 27 centimeters in length and 19 centimeters in width. A medium weight macroporous polypropylene mesh is introduced through an eight port and unrolled using a suture technique. The mesh is secured to the Cooper's ligament and pubis with several interrupted vicryl sutures to prevent it from riding up. No drains are placed, and the patient is discharged on postoperative day one.
The mesh is visible covering the port sites, indicating successful placement and coverage of the surgical area.
